hook: You already know how to evaluate an equation like $y = 2x + 1$ for a single value of $x$ — just plug in and calculate. But what if you want to see the entire behavior of the equation, not just one point? A single output tells you almost nothing about shape or direction. How could you use repeated substitution to build a picture of the full line?
concept: Graphing by table means you choose several $x$-values, compute the corresponding $y$-values using the equation, record the pairs as ordered pairs $(x, y)$, plot those points on the coordinate plane, and draw a straight line through them. Formally, for a linear equation $y = mx + b$, each input $x$ from your chosen set produces exactly one output $y$, creating a set of points that all lie on the same line.
anatomy: A table of values for graphing has four distinct parts working together. First, the equation itself — for example $y = 3x - 2$ — is the rule that connects inputs to outputs. Second, the chosen $x$-values are the inputs you select (typically a small set like $-2, -1, 0, 1, 2$ centered around zero for convenience). Third, the computed $y$-values are the outputs you get by substituting each $x$ into the equation. Fourth, the ordered pairs $(x, y)$ are the coordinate points you actually plot; each pair is a location on the coordinate plane, and together they reveal the line.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying the equation we need to graph. Our job is to find several points that satisfy this equation, so we will build a table by choosing a handful of $x$-values — here we will use $x = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$ — because they are easy to compute with and give us enough points to draw a reliable line.

We substitute each chosen $x$-value into $y = 2x - 1$ and simplify. Notice that every time $x$ increases by $1$, the $y$-value increases by $2$, which is exactly the slope — this is a good internal check that your arithmetic is consistent.

We write each row of the table as an ordered pair $(x, y)$. These five coordinate pairs are the points we will plot on the coordinate plane, and every one of them is an exact solution to the original equation.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Plot each point, then draw a straight line through all five.}
worked_example.description_latex: We plot all five ordered pairs on the coordinate plane and then draw a single straight line passing through all of them. If the points are not collinear, that is a signal to recheck your substitution — a linear equation always produces points that line up perfectly.

partial_example.description_latex: Here is a new equation to graph by table. We will use the same strategy: pick $x$-values, substitute, record ordered pairs, and plot. We will use $x = -1, 0, 1, 2$.
The first two rows have been completed for you. Substitute $x = 1$ and $x = 2$ into $y = -3x + 4$ to fill in the missing $y$-values — remember to apply the negative sign on the slope carefully.

Using the completed table, write all four ordered pairs and plot them on the coordinate plane, then draw the line. Notice the $y$-values are decreasing as $x$ increases — this reflects the negative slope $-3$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Complete a table of values for $y = x + 3$ using $x = -1, 0, 1, 2$ and list the ordered pairs.
Make a table of values for $y = 2x - 4$ using $x = -1, 0, 1, 2, 3$ and describe the direction of the line.
practice_problems.answer_latex: (-1,\,-6),\;(0,\,-4),\;(1,\,-2),\;(2,\,0),\;(3,\,2);\; \text{the line rises left to right (positive slope)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Build a table for $y = -x + 5$ using $x = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$ and identify the $y$-intercept from your table.
Graph $y = \dfrac{1}{2}x - 3$ by completing a table with $x = -2, 0, 2, 4$ (choose $x$-values that avoid fractions) and find where the line crosses the $x$-axis.

A cell-phone plan charges a flat fee of $\$10$ plus $\$0.05$ per text message. This is modeled by $C = 0.05t + 10$, where $t$ is the number of texts. Complete a table for $t = 0, 20, 40, 60, 80$ and determine the cost for $60$ texts.

common_mistakes: Students often reverse the order of coordinates when plotting, writing $(y, x)$ instead of $(x, y)$. Because the horizontal axis is always $x$ and the vertical axis is always $y$, you must write the input first and the output second in every ordered pair.

When the equation has a negative coefficient like $y = -2x + 5$, students frequently forget to apply the negative sign during substitution — for example writing $y = 2(3) + 5 = 11$ instead of $y = -2(3) + 5 = -1$. Always carry the negative sign with the coefficient before you multiply.

Students sometimes choose only two points for their table and connect them, then are thrown off when a small arithmetic error goes undetected. Using at least three to five points lets you spot mistakes immediately because a correct linear equation must produce points that are perfectly collinear — if one point looks off, recheck that row's calculation.
untested_variants: Graphing equations written in standard form $Ax + By = C$ (e.g., $2x + 3y = 6$) where students must first solve for $y$ before building a table.
Equations with fractional slopes where the recommended strategy is to choose $x$-values that are multiples of the denominator to keep all $y$-values as integers.
Horizontal lines of the form $y = c$ and vertical lines of the form $x = c$, where one variable is absent entirely and every row of the table produces the same output.
Using a table to graph a linear inequality (e.g., $y < 2x + 1$) and then deciding which side of the line to shade.
